narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 57% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in academic records, test scores, and backgrounds of past students, focusing on key indicators of bar success without considering gender or race. In your case, the three strongest factors shaping this outcome were your LSAT score, your third-year grade decile in school, and your first-year grade decile. Most influential was your LSAT score of 25, which is notably below the average of about 37 for students who passed the bar; this pulled the prediction strongly toward expecting failure, as lower scores like yours have historically correlated with tougher bar outcomes. Counterbalancing that somewhat was your third-year decile of 4, higher than the typical 5.9 average among bar passers, which nudged the model toward seeing potential for success. Your first-year decile of 1, however, stood out as much lower than the 6 average for successful students, reinforcing the risk of failure. While your upper-middle family income aligns well with many who passed (where it's the most common bracket) and your part-time status is less typical (only about 7% of passers were part-time), these played smaller roles compared to your grades and LSAT. Overall, your profile showed strengths in later-year performance and background that helped, but they weren't enough to offset the early academic and test score concerns when stacked against admitted students, who generally had stronger LSATs around 37 and higher deciles from the start. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, as that's the single biggest lever here, and reflect on strategies to improve early-grade performance in law school if given another chance. Building a narrative around your upward trend from first to third year could also strengthen future applications, perhaps with letters highlighting that resilience. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by targeting these areas—reach out to advisors for personalized prep plans, and don't hesitate to reapply once you've addressed the key gaps. Your part-time path shows determination, and with targeted improvements, the model would likely see you differently next time.
